beats.text:
Many are the hours in which I have pondered upon the story that is set forth in the following pages.

I trust that my instincts are not awry when they prompt me to leave the account, in simplicity, as it was handed to me.

The MS. itself — you must picture me, when first it was given into my care, turning it over, curiously.

A small book; but thick, and all — save the last few pages — filled with a quaint but legible handwriting, writ very close.
I have the queer, faint, [wave]pit-water smell[/wave] of it in my nostrils now as I write, and my fingers have subconscious memories of the soft, cloggy feel of the long-damp pages.

I read, and, in reading, lifted the Curtains of the Impossible that blind the mind, and looked out into the unknown.

[i]— William Hope Hodgson, December 17, 1907[/i]

Right away in the west of Ireland lies a tiny hamlet called Kraighten.

Far around there spreads a waste of bleak and totally inhospitable country — bare and unpeopled, the very earth scarcely covering the rock that lies beneath it.

Yet, in spite of its desolation, my friend Tonnison and I had elected to spend our vacation there.

He had stumbled on the place by mere chance, and discovered the possibilities for the angler in a small and unnamed river that runs past the outskirts of the little village.
No map I had consulted showed either village or stream. They seemed to have entirely escaped observation — as though they might never exist for all that the average guide tells one.

We arrived in Kraighten one warm evening, thoroughly tired and somewhat bad tempered. The village people eyed me curiously, but not in any unfriendly manner — though none of them ventured a word.

They did not know a word of English.

Thus we spent several days, having splendid sport and first-rate appetites. We were pleased to find how friendly the villagers were inclined to be.

It was on a Sunday that we made the great discovery.

That day we laid aside our rods and set off in the opposite direction from our usual path.

And then, without any warning whatsoever, the river we had followed so confidently [shake]came to an abrupt end[/shake] — vanishing into the earth.

"Good Lord," I said. "Who ever would have thought of this?"

Tonnison stared at the place where the river disappeared. "Let us go on a bit," he said. "It may reappear again — anyhow, it is worth investigating."

For perhaps a mile we moved onward; then Tonnison stopped and shaded his eyes.

"See — isn't that mist, over there to the right?" And he indicated with his hand a faint column of [wave]haze[/wave], rising high into the quiet air, upon which the sun shone, causing innumerable rainbows.

Down the sloping bank we made our way, and entered among the trees and shrubberies.

The bushes were matted, and the trees overhung us, so that the place was disagreeably gloomy. Many of the trees were fruit trees, and here and there one could trace the signs of a long-departed cultivation.

What a [pulse color=#2a3a2a]wild[/pulse] place it was — so dismal and somber.

Somehow, as we went forward, a sense of the silent loneliness and desertion of the old garden grew upon me, and I felt shivery. One could imagine things lurking among the tangled bushes.

Suddenly, we came to a halt. Through the trees there had grown upon our ears a distant sound — continuous and harsh. A sort of [wave]droning roar[/wave], seeming to come from far below.

I experienced a queer, indescribable little feeling of nervousness.

We came out from among the trees on to a great open space, where — not six paces in front of us — yawned the mouth of a tremendous [shake]chasm[/shake].

From its depths there rose a continuous, mistlike spray, and the thunder of falling water.

Together, we looked down through a boil of spray at a monster cataract of frothing water that burst, spouting, from the side of the chasm — nearly a hundred feet below.

The sight was so unexpectedly grand and [pulse color=#3a1a3a]eerie[/pulse].

I looked up and across to the further side, and saw something towering up among the spray — a fragment of a great ruin, perched almost at the extreme end of a huge spur of rock that jutted out some fifty feet over the abyss.

The jagged mass was literally suspended in midair.

We walked out onto the projecting arm of rock.

I must confess to having felt an intolerable sense of [shake]terror[/shake] as I looked down from that dizzy perch into the unknown depths below — into the deeps from which there rose, ever, the thunder of the falling water.

The ruin appeared a portion of the outer wall of some prodigious structure, so thick and substantially built. Yet what it was doing in such a position I could by no means conjecture.

Where was the rest of the house, or castle, or whatever there had been?

I heard a cry from Tonnison — he was shouting my name, excitedly.

I hurried back along the promontory to find him standing within a small excavation he had made among the débris, brushing the dirt from something that looked like a [pulse color=#5a4a20]book[/pulse].

He handed it to me. Its pages were closely filled with neat, old-fashioned writing — quite legible, save in one portion, where many of the pages were muddied and crumpled, as though the book had been doubled back at that part.

Curiously enough, it was fairly dry.

We gave up searching and went back along the rock, making our way through the old garden.

Then, from the wood on our left, there came a strange [wave][color=#4a3a2a]wailing[/color][/wave] noise. It appeared to float through the trees, and there was a rustle of stirring leaves, and then silence.

Tonnison put his hand on my shoulder. "Let us get out of here."

I glanced around nervously as we went — but saw nothing, save the quiet branches and trunks and the tangled bushes. Yet I had a horrible feeling that we were [shake]not alone[/shake].

"I would not spend the night in that place for all the wealth that the world holds," Tonnison said as we came back out onto the bare rock of the countryside. "There is something unholy — diabolical — about it."

Once, as we moved away, there seemed to come again a distant sound of [wave]wailing[/wave]. I told myself it was the wind.

Yet the evening was breathless.

It was two hours later when we reached the tent. Supper over, Tonnison asked me to read the manuscript aloud.

"And mind," he cautioned, "don't go skipping half the book."

And there, seated in the opening of our little tent, I began the strange tale of [pulse color=#8b0000][i]The House on the Borderland[/i][/pulse].
